reason: The item cleanly tests a single engineering judgment boundary: equal IDLH-based safety distances do not entail equivalent within-zone toxic profiles or dose risk, especially when gas species yields differ. Decoys are plausible and each fails primarily by conflating a boundary metric with internal concentrations, yields, or source peaks, while correct options consistently articulate the within-zone profile/dose caveat. Discrimination is strong for experts because it penalizes common overinterpretations of dispersion “safety distance” outputs and requires boundary-aware reasoning rather than rote recall.

The item cleanly tests the core engineering judgment that safety distance is threshold-based (IDLH-normalized concentration) rather than mass-yield-based, with HF’s low IDLH providing a sharp discriminator against the intuitive but wrong “largest quantity governs” heuristic. It has strong separation potential because an overconfident respondent will default to CO abundance, while an expert will invoke the concentration/IDLH ratio and explicitly qualify scope to burning scenarios. Minor risk remains that, as a short-answer item, grading can be sensitive to how explicitly the solver states the “all burning simulation cases” boundary versus making an over-broad generalization.
